preamble: The transformation from the voiced alveolar affricate [dz] to the voiced palatal approximant [j] is a historically attested phonetic shift, primarily resulting from processes of lenition and palatalization. This change involves the affricate losing its stop component and becoming a smoother, less obstructed approximant, while simultaneously shifting its point of articulation further back towards the hard palate.
phoneticEffects: Lenition (Affricate to Approximant), Palatalization (Alveolar to Palatal)

examples.note: Proto-*dz- shifted to /j-/ in Northeastern Jingpo dialects, particularly before front vowels.

In some varieties of Gulf Arabic, including Emirati Arabic, the voiced postalveolar affricate [d͡ʒ] (often a reflex of Old Arabic /g/) undergoes lenition to the voiced palatal approximant [j]. This is a synchronic phonological process that also reflects a historical change.

sources: Phonological conditioning of affricate variability in Emirati Arabic - ResearchGate.
Phonological conditioning of affricate variability in Emirati Arabic - Cambridge University Press & Assessment.
What processes of lenition in historical phonology exemplify affrication? - Linguistics Stack Exchange.
